_meta.description: Quiz questions for AMPH Academy modules. Each quiz maps to a module's final lesson (knowledge check). Questions test PPC concepts covered in the preceding lessons.

quizzes.title: Onboarding Knowledge Check
quizzes.description: Test your understanding of the AMPH Academy platform and Amazon PPC basics covered in the Onboarding module.

questions.question: What does PPC stand for in the context of Amazon advertising?
questions.optionA: Pay Per Click
questions.optionB: Product Promotion Campaign
questions.optionC: Paid Product Catalog
questions.optionD: Performance Per Cost
questions.explanation: PPC stands for Pay Per Click — you only pay when a shopper clicks on your ad, not when it's displayed.

questions.question: Which Amazon ad type appears within search results and on product detail pages?
questions.optionA: Sponsored Brands
questions.optionB: Sponsored Display
questions.optionC: Sponsored Products
questions.optionD: Display Ads
questions.explanation: Sponsored Products are the most common ad type — they appear in search results and on product detail pages, promoting individual listings.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of Amazon PPC for sellers?
questions.optionA: Free product listings on Amazon
questions.optionB: Increased organic search ranking guaranteed
questions.optionC: Getting products in front of shoppers who are actively searching
questions.optionD: Automatic review generation
questions.explanation: Amazon PPC puts your products in front of shoppers who are actively searching for related items, giving you visibility at the moment of purchase intent.

questions.question: What does your Amazon PPC bid actually control?
questions.optionA: The exact price you'll pay for every click
questions.optionB: A starting point that placement adjustments and your bid strategy then act on to set the real ceiling
questions.optionC: A price that never changes once the campaign is live
questions.optionD: A guaranteed discount of one cent below the next bidder
questions.explanation: Your entered bid is the base. Placement adjustments raise it for specific placements, and your bid strategy setting decides whether the result is a hard ceiling (Fixed Bids, Dynamic Down Only) or something Amazon can raise further for likely conversions (Dynamic Up and Down). The safe habit isn't memorizing an auction formula, it's setting a defensible maximum bid and then watching your realized CPC in the reports.

questions.question: What should you do before launching your first Amazon PPC campaign?
questions.optionA: Set the highest possible bids to win all auctions
questions.optionB: Make sure your product listing is optimized with good images and copy
questions.optionC: Wait until you have at least 100 reviews
questions.optionD: Only target the most expensive keywords
questions.explanation: Your product listing is the foundation of PPC success. Even the best ads can't convert if the listing is poor. Optimize images, title, bullets, and price before spending on ads.

quizzes.title: PPC Foundations Knowledge Check
quizzes.description: Verify your mastery of the core PPC metrics — CPC, CTR, ACoS, TACoS, and RoAS — the building blocks of every Amazon advertising decision.

questions.question: If you spend $200 on a keyword and receive 160 clicks, what is your CPC?
questions.explanation: CPC = Total Spend / Total Clicks = $200 / 160 = $1.25. This is your average cost for each click received.

questions.question: Your ad receives 3,000 impressions and 90 clicks. What is the CTR?
questions.explanation: CTR = Clicks / Impressions = 90 / 3,000 = 0.03 = 3.0%. A 3% CTR is solid for exact match keywords on Amazon.

questions.question: What does ACoS measure?
questions.optionA: The percentage of clicks that result in a sale
questions.optionB: The ratio of ad spend to ad revenue
questions.optionC: The total advertising cost of your entire account
questions.optionD: The average cost per sale across all campaigns
questions.explanation: ACoS (Advertising Cost of Sales) = Ad Spend / Ad Revenue. It tells you what percentage of your ad revenue goes toward ad costs. A 25% ACoS means you spend $0.25 on ads for every $1 in ad revenue.

questions.question: How is TACoS different from ACoS?
questions.optionA: TACoS includes organic sales in the denominator, ACoS only uses ad revenue
questions.optionB: TACoS is always higher than ACoS
questions.optionC: They measure the same thing — TACoS is just the newer name
questions.optionD: TACoS measures click cost while ACoS measures revenue
questions.explanation: TACoS = Ad Spend / Total Revenue (organic + ads), while ACoS = Ad Spend / Ad Revenue only. TACoS gives you a holistic view of how ads impact your total business, not just the direct ad-attributed revenue.

questions.question: A keyword has an ACoS of 45% and your product profit margin is 30%. What should you consider?
questions.optionA: The keyword is performing well — keep it as-is
questions.optionB: You're likely losing money on this keyword since ACoS exceeds profit margin
questions.optionC: The profit margin doesn't matter for ACoS calculations
questions.optionD: You should double your bid to get more clicks
questions.explanation: When ACoS exceeds your profit margin, you're spending more on ads than you make in profit. A 45% ACoS on a 30% margin means you lose 15 percentage points on every ad-attributed sale. Consider lowering bids, adding negatives, or pausing.

questions.question: If your RoAS is 4.0x, what does that mean?
questions.optionA: You get 4 clicks for every dollar spent
questions.optionB: You earn $4 in total revenue for every $1 spent on ads
questions.optionC: You earn $4 in ad revenue for every $1 spent on ads
questions.optionD: Your ACoS is 4%
questions.explanation: RoAS = Ad Revenue / Ad Spend. A 4.0x RoAS means for every $1 in ad spend, you generate $4 in ad-attributed revenue. It's the inverse of ACoS: RoAS = 1 / ACoS.

questions.question: Why is it dangerous to evaluate CPC in isolation?
questions.optionA: CPC is not an important metric
questions.optionB: A low CPC on irrelevant keywords can still waste money if those clicks don't convert
questions.optionC: Amazon doesn't report CPC accurately
questions.optionD: CPC should always be as low as possible regardless of other metrics
questions.explanation: Cheap clicks from irrelevant search terms can have a low CPC but infinite effective cost if they never convert. Always evaluate CPC alongside CVR, ACoS, and relevance.

questions.question: What is the relationship between CTR and CPC in Amazon's advertising system?
questions.optionA: They are completely independent metrics
questions.optionB: Higher CTR can lead to lower CPC because Amazon rewards relevant ads
questions.optionC: Higher CTR always means higher CPC
questions.optionD: CTR only affects ad placement, not CPC
questions.explanation: Amazon's algorithm uses CTR as a quality signal. Higher CTR indicates relevance, which can lead to better placements at lower costs — creating a virtuous cycle of better performance.

quizzes.title: Campaign Architecture Knowledge Check
quizzes.description: Test your understanding of Sponsored Products, Sponsored Brands, Sponsored Display, and how to structure campaigns for maximum performance.

questions.question: Which Amazon ad type allows you to promote a portfolio of products with a custom headline and logo?
questions.optionA: Sponsored Products
questions.optionB: Sponsored Brands
questions.optionC: Sponsored Display
questions.optionD: Amazon DSP
questions.explanation: Sponsored Brands (formerly Headline Search Ads) feature your brand logo, a custom headline, and a collection of products. They appear at the top of search results and drive brand awareness.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of a manual targeting campaign over auto campaigns?
questions.optionA: Manual campaigns are always cheaper
questions.optionB: Manual campaigns give you precise control over which keywords and match types you target
questions.optionC: Manual campaigns generate more impressions automatically
questions.optionD: Manual campaigns don't require any keyword research
questions.explanation: Manual targeting gives you explicit control over keyword selection and match types, allowing you to strategically bid on high-converting terms and exclude low-performers with precision.

questions.question: When should you use broad match keywords in your campaigns?
questions.optionA: When you want to discover new search terms that convert for your product
questions.optionB: When you want the most precise control over ad spend
questions.optionC: When you want to minimize irrelevant clicks
questions.optionD: Broad match should never be used in Amazon PPC
questions.explanation: Broad match is your discovery tool — it triggers your ad for variations and related searches, helping you find new converting search terms. Always monitor the search term report and convert high-performers to phrase or exact match.

questions.question: What is the recommended campaign structure for Sponsored Products?
questions.optionA: One campaign with all keywords in a single ad group
questions.optionB: Separate campaigns for auto, broad, phrase, and exact match types
questions.optionC: Only use auto campaigns — manual is too complicated
questions.optionD: One campaign per individual keyword
questions.explanation: Segmenting by match type (auto, broad, phrase, exact) into separate campaigns gives you granular budget and bid control at each stage of the keyword lifecycle — from discovery to optimization.

questions.question: Sponsored Display ads can target which of the following audiences?
questions.optionA: Only shoppers who searched for your exact keywords
questions.optionB: Views (shoppers who viewed your product) and Purchases (shoppers who bought your product)
questions.optionC: Only shoppers who added your product to cart
questions.optionD: Only shoppers on competing product detail pages
questions.explanation: Sponsored Display offers powerful remarketing: you can retarget shoppers who viewed your product (Views) or who purchased from you (Purchases) with different messaging strategies for each audience.

questions.question: What is a common mistake when setting daily budgets for multiple campaigns?
questions.optionA: Setting budgets too low, causing campaigns to pause mid-day and lose high-converting evening traffic
questions.optionB: Setting different budgets for different campaigns
questions.optionC: Using the same bid for all keywords in a campaign
questions.optionD: Running auto campaigns alongside manual campaigns
questions.explanation: Under-budgeting causes campaigns to stop running before the day ends. Amazon shoppers are active 24/7, and you may miss peak converting hours. Monitor spend patterns and set budgets that allow full-day coverage for proven campaigns.

quizzes.title: Bidding Lab Knowledge Check
quizzes.description: Verify your understanding of bid strategies, placement adjustments, and how to optimize bids for profitability.

questions.question: What does 'Dynamic Bids - Down Only' do?
questions.optionA: Automatically increases your bid for placements likely to convert
questions.optionB: Automatically decreases your bid for placements unlikely to convert, but never raises it
questions.optionC: Sets all your bids to the same amount
questions.optionD: Dynamically adjusts bids based on time of day
questions.explanation: Dynamic Bids - Down Only tells Amazon to reduce your bid when the placement is unlikely to convert, saving you money on low-value clicks. It never raises your bid above your set amount, making it the most conservative strategy.

questions.question: You're running a campaign with 50% placement adjustment for 'Top of Search'. If your default bid is $1.00, what is your maximum bid for top of search?
questions.explanation: A 50% placement adjustment increases your bid by 50% for that placement: $1.00 + ($1.00 × 0.50) = $1.50 maximum bid for top of search positions.

questions.question: When should you use 'Dynamic Bids - Up and Down'?
questions.optionA: When you want to minimize ad spend at all costs
questions.optionB: When your campaigns are profitable and you want Amazon to increase bids for high-conversion opportunities
questions.optionC: Only for auto campaigns
questions.optionD: When launching a new campaign with no historical data
questions.explanation: Up and Down bidding works best on proven campaigns with good ACoS. Amazon will raise bids for placements likely to convert and lower them for poor ones. Avoid it on new or unprofitable campaigns.

questions.question: What is the risk of setting your default bid too high in a manual campaign?
questions.optionA: Your ads won't show at all
questions.optionB: You may win clicks for expensive, low-converting search terms and rapidly drain your budget
questions.optionC: Amazon will automatically lower your bid
questions.optionD: Your ACoS will decrease automatically
questions.explanation: Overbidding wins you more auctions — including low-quality ones. You'll burn through budget on expensive clicks from irrelevant or low-converting search terms, driving up ACoS and reducing overall profitability.

questions.question: Your keyword has a CPC of $2.50, CVR of 8%, and your product price is $30 with 30% profit margin. What is your approximate ACoS?
questions.explanation: Cost per order = CPC / CVR = $2.50 / 0.08 = $31.25. ACoS = Ad Spend / Ad Revenue = $31.25 / $30 = 104.2%. Since this exceeds 100%, you're paying more per order than the product price — this keyword is extremely unprofitable.

quizzes.title: Search Term Triage Knowledge Check
quizzes.description: Test your understanding of search term analysis, negative keywords, and how to optimize campaigns through strategic triage decisions.

questions.question: What is a search term in Amazon PPC?
questions.optionA: The keyword you bid on in your campaign
questions.optionB: The actual shopper query that triggered your ad
questions.optionC: The title of your product listing
questions.optionD: The search algorithm Amazon uses
questions.explanation: A search term is the actual query a shopper typed — which may differ from your keyword. For example, if you bid on the broad keyword 'water bottle', a search term might be 'insulated water bottle for gym'. This distinction is critical for optimization.

questions.question: When should you add a search term as an exact negative keyword?
questions.optionA: When the term has high spend and zero sales
questions.optionB: When the term has a high ACoS above your target
questions.optionC: When the term is completely irrelevant to your product
questions.optionD: All of the above are valid reasons
questions.explanation: All three scenarios justify adding an exact negative: high spend with zero sales is pure waste, ACoS above target means the term is unprofitable, and completely irrelevant terms waste budget and hurt CTR.

questions.question: What is the difference between negative exact and negative phrase match types?
questions.optionA: They work the same way for negatives
questions.optionB: Negative exact blocks only that specific search term; negative phrase blocks the term and any searches containing it
questions.optionC: Negative phrase is more restrictive than negative exact
questions.optionD: There is no phrase match option for negatives
questions.explanation: Negative exact match blocks your ad from showing for only that specific search term. Negative phrase match blocks your ad for any search that contains the term, which is broader. Use negative exact for precision and negative phrase to block entire categories of unwanted searches.

questions.question: A search term has 500 impressions, 2 clicks, and 0 orders with $3.50 spend. What triage action should you consider?
questions.optionA: Keep bidding — it might convert with more clicks
questions.optionB: Increase your bid to get more visibility
questions.optionC: Add as a negative keyword — the CTR is very low and it's spending without converting
questions.optionD: Do nothing — 2 clicks is not enough data
questions.explanation: CTR of 0.4% (2/500) is extremely low and the term has $3.50 spend with zero return. This indicates the search term is irrelevant to your product. Adding it as a negative prevents further waste.

questions.question: You find a high-converting search term in your broad match campaign. What is the recommended next step?
questions.optionA: Leave it in the broad campaign and increase the broad bid
questions.optionB: Add it as an exact match keyword in a separate exact campaign with a higher bid
questions.optionC: Remove it from the broad campaign immediately
questions.optionD: Add it as a negative keyword to prevent broad from triggering for it
questions.explanation: The best practice is to graduate high-converting search terms to an exact match campaign with a higher bid. This gives you precise control and ensures you win auctions for this proven term. Optionally, add it as a negative in the broad campaign to avoid competing with yourself.

questions.question: Why is regular search term analysis essential for PPC profitability?
questions.optionA: Amazon requires weekly reports to keep your account active
questions.optionB: It helps you discover irrelevant terms wasting budget and find new converting terms to target explicitly
questions.optionC: It automatically optimizes your bids for you
questions.optionD: It replaces the need for bid strategy adjustments
questions.explanation: Regular search term analysis is the single highest-ROI activity in PPC management. It lets you cut waste (irrelevant terms draining budget) and capture opportunity (converting terms you should bid on explicitly). This is the core loop: discover → triage → optimize → repeat.
